title: When container is joined to multiple networks, PublishedPort cannot be connected to from host.
body:
### Issue Description

Network isolation doesn't seem to work consistently

Testing this on RHEL 9.5, podman 5.2.2, rootful, aardvark, firewall_driver="nftables", firewalld is disabled and there is no other source of firewall rules outside of podman. Quadlets are being used to launch the containers.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

In my test case I have 4 basic nginx containers listing on 8080, each connected to their own network ie: nginx1, nginx2, etc.  I have a single traefik (reverse proxy) container which is connected to all 4.

----- Example nginx quadlet:
[Unit]
Description=nginx1

[Container]
ContainerName=nginx1
Image=localhost/nginx1/alpine-nginx
AutoUpdate=registry
Pull=newer
Network=nginx1

[Service]
Restart=always

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target

----- Example traefik quadlet:
[Unit]
Description=traefik

[Container]
ContainerName=traefik
Image=docker.io/library/traefik:latest
Volume=traefik.etc:/etc/traefik:ro,Z
Volume=traefik.certs:/certs:Z
PublishPort=80:80
PublishPort=443:443

AutoUpdate=registry
Pull=newer
Network=traefik
Network=nginx1
Network=nginx2
Network=nginx3
Network=nginx4

[Service]
Restart=always

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target

### Describe the results you received

The result is that I cannot connect to port 80/443 of the traefik container from the podman host.

From within the traefik container I can always curl each nginx container by name successfully

If the nginx* networks are instead created with --internal, and an additional network for traefik is created for the traefik container, then it all works, seemingly consistently. However that's not a solution because in some cases (for example wordpress) a container may need internet access to function.

If I use a single network for everything, that also works.

I also tried a suggestion that the first network a container attaches to is significant and effects how ports are published. So I tried joining traefik only to the "traefik" network at launch, then `podman network attach` the rest manually. This also doesn't seem to work, at least not consistently.

In some cases the original solution seems to work, occasionally, but I haven't been able to fully pin this behavior down. I'm much less certain about this, but I'm pretty sure I've had occasions where I bring everything up and it works, at least for awhile. I've compared nftables list ruleset when it's working vs when it's not and I don't see any difference. I've had cases where I've network attached things, and it stayed working. However the general rule is that it doesn't work.

### Describe the results you expected

What I want to accomplish is for each pod or container to be able to communicate with the reverse proxy, but not with each other.

### podman info output

```yaml
[ec2-user@ip-172-30-2-160 nginx1]$ sudo podman info
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

AWS

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body: Can you provide full nftables rules (`sudo nft list table inet netavark`) for a working configuration and broken configuration?

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).
@Luap99 Potentially a routing issue, given metrics cause it to stop reproducing?

> [@Luap99](https://github.com/Luap99) Potentially a routing issue, given metrics cause it to stop reproducing?

It's inconsistent. I'm not convinced the metric has anything to do with it. On ubuntu it seems to be working consistently and all networks are metric 100. The case earlier on rhel when I set one of the networks to metric 1 it worked, until I restarted, then it didn't despite the network still having a metric of 1. I also tried the same test again, tearing everything down, making one network metric 1, and it didn't work. Currently I have the metrics all set to 100 and it's working but with long delays.  I tried disabling ipv6 entirely just in case it had something to do with that, it doesn't.

Examining an nft monitor trace shows that the issue appears to be caused by asymetric routing.  When a packet comes in from the outside, it is dnatted to whatever network comes first in the nft chain. Traefik then responds on a random interface, if that interface happens to be the one it entered on, it works, if not it doesn't.

Here's an example of a failed transaction
```
trace id f0b0bef7 inet trace trace-prerouting packet: iif "eth0" ether saddr 0a:02:88:4d:ff:df ether daddr 0a:ff:f3:a9:51:37 ip saddr <myip> ip daddr 172.30.2.160 ip dscp cs0 ip ecn not-ect ip ttl 51 ip id 59539 ip protocol tcp ip length 60 tcp sport 49364 tcp dport 443 tcp flags == syn tcp window 64240
trace id f0b0bef7 inet trace trace-prerouting rule tcp dport { 80, 443 } meta nftrace set 1 (verdict continue)
trace id f0b0bef7 inet trace trace-prerouting rule tcp dport { 80, 443 } meta nftrace set 1 (verdict continue)
trace id f0b0bef7 inet trace trace-prerouting policy accept
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark PREROUTING packet: iif "eth0" ether saddr 0a:02:88:4d:ff:df ether daddr 0a:ff:f3:a9:51:37 ip saddr <myip> ip daddr 172.30.2.160 ip dscp cs0 ip ecn not-ect ip ttl 51 ip id 59539 ip protocol tcp ip length 60 tcp sport 49364 tcp dport 443 tcp flags == syn tcp window 64240
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark PREROUTING rule fib daddr type local jump NETAVARK-HOSTPORT-DNAT (verdict jump NETAVARK-HOSTPORT-DNAT)
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark NETAVARK-HOSTPORT-DNAT rule tcp dport 443 jump nv_567a82cd_10_89_3_0_nm24_dnat (verdict jump nv_567a82cd_10_89_3_0_nm24_dnat)
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark nv_567a82cd_10_89_3_0_nm24_dnat rule tcp dport 443 dnat ip to 10.89.3.3:443 (verdict accept)
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark FORWARD packet: iif "eth0" oif "podman4" ether saddr 0a:02:88:4d:ff:df ether daddr 0a:ff:f3:a9:51:37 ip saddr <myip> ip daddr 10.89.3.3 ip dscp cs0 ip ecn not-ect ip ttl 50 ip id 59539 ip protocol tcp ip length 60 tcp sport 49364 tcp dport 443 tcp flags == syn tcp window 64240
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark FORWARD rule jump NETAVARK-ISOLATION-1 (verdict jump NETAVARK-ISOLATION-1)
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark FORWARD rule ip saddr 10.89.2.0/24 accept (verdict continue)
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark FORWARD policy accept
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark POSTROUTING packet: iif "eth0" oif "podman4" ether saddr 0a:02:88:4d:ff:df ether daddr 0a:ff:f3:a9:51:37 ip saddr <myip> ip daddr 10.89.3.3 ip dscp cs0 ip ecn not-ect ip ttl 50 ip id 59539 ip protocol tcp ip length 60 tcp sport 49364 tcp dport 443 tcp flags == syn tcp window 64240
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark POSTROUTING rule ip saddr 10.89.2.0/24 jump nv_bd5be297_10_89_2_0_nm24 (verdict continue)
trace id f0b0bef7 inet netavark POSTROUTING policy accept
trace id a7fd4abb inet trace trace-prerouting packet: iif "podman3" ether saddr c2:e6:86:80:80:35 ether daddr e6:b7:c2:ac:41:f4 ip saddr 10.89.3.3 ip daddr <myip> ip dscp cs0 ip ecn not-ect ip ttl 64 ip id 0 ip protocol tcp ip length 60 tcp sport 443 tcp dport 49364 tcp flags == 0x12 tcp window 31856
trace id a7fd4abb inet trace trace-prerouting rule tcp sport { 80, 443 } meta nftrace set 1 (verdict continue)
trace id a7fd4abb inet trace trace-prerouting rule tcp dport { 80, 443 } meta nftrace set 1 (verdict continue)
trace id a7fd4abb inet trace trace-prerouting policy accept

```

ok.. I found the difference.  Ubuntu has /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter = 2.  Redhat sets it to 0, however the podman interfaces have it set to 1. Therefore this asymetric routing situation causes the packets to get dropped by the kernel.  Setting it to 2 seems to resolve the issue.

This was fixed in netavark v1.11 with https://github.com/containers/netavark/pull/962/commits/9a73a52c5fd1f20e327a01e29e7599beca668fc1 which you supposedly are already using. So if the podman bridge really has rp_filter 1 then something weird must be going on.

grep rp_filter /usr/lib/sysctl.d/50-redhat.conf
```
net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 1
net.ipv4.conf.*.rp_filter = 1
-net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter
```
grep rp_filter /usr/lib/sysctl.d/50-default.conf
```
net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 2
net.ipv4.conf.*.rp_filter = 2
-net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter
```

grep -H '^' /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/*/rp_filter
```
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter:0
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/default/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/eth0/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/lo/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman1/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman2/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman3/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman4/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth0/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth1/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth2/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth3/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth4/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth5/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth6/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth7/rp_filter:1
```

And that happens right after you start the container? I don't see why our sysctl write would not be happening.

It is clearly getting set:
```
$ podman --log-level debug run --network bridge quay.io/libpod/testimage:20241011 true |& grep rp_filter
[DEBUG netavark::network::core_utils] Setting sysctl value for /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman0/rp_filter to 2
[DEBUG netavark::network::core_utils] Setting sysctl value for /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/eth0/rp_filter to 2
```

I added debug to my traefik quadlet

```
$ sudo journalctl -u traefik | grep rp_filter
May 02 10:43:04 toolsdr traefik[1398]: [DEBUG netavark::network::core_utils] Setting sysctl value for /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman1/rp_filter to 2
May 02 10:43:04 toolsdr traefik[1398]: [DEBUG netavark::network::core_utils] Setting sysctl value for /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/eth0/rp_filter to 2
May 02 10:43:05 toolsdr traefik[1398]: [DEBUG netavark::network::core_utils] Setting sysctl value for /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman2/rp_filter to 2
May 02 10:43:05 toolsdr traefik[1398]: [DEBUG netavark::network::core_utils] Setting sysctl value for /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/eth1/rp_filter to 2
May 02 10:43:05 toolsdr traefik[1398]: [DEBUG netavark::network::core_utils] Setting sysctl value for /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman3/rp_filter to 2
May 02 10:43:05 toolsdr traefik[1398]: [DEBUG netavark::network::core_utils] Setting sysctl value for /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/eth2/rp_filter to 2

$ date
Fri May  2 10:44:34 AM CDT 2025

$ grep -H '^' /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/*/rp_filter
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter:0
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/default/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/eth0/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/lo/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman1/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman2/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/podman3/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth0/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth1/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth2/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth3/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth4/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth5/rp_filter:1
/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/veth6/rp_filter:1
```

Does it work when you run normally via podman run outside of quadlet/systemd? At this point I don't think this is a upstream issue, we set the sysctl (you could strace the process to be sure) so I don't see what we should do here. If your sysctl setting are geeing overridden again then I don't see what podman/netavark can do.

Because you are using RHEL I would suggest you file a bug in RHEL (which btw you should always do since RHEL is using older pakcages and we upstream only supports the latest)

I tested it directly and get the same result
`sudo podman run -d --name mybox --network traefik alpine sleep infinity`

But I agree, I'll file a bug with rhel

Actually, I forgot to mention, I see the same behavior on Ubuntu. In Ubuntu's case they set rp_filter to 2, so they don't have the same issue by default. However, if I set rp_filter to 1 in sysctl, podman will try to set it to 2, but the interfaces stay at 1 and the same problem is then observed.
